draft.introduction: Mark 9:14–29 recounts the healing of the boy with an unclean spirit, immediately after the Transfiguration. The father’s cry—“I believe; help my unbelief!”—is among the most psychologically honest moments in Scripture. From Codex Sinaiticus.

translation.text: And it has often cast him into fire and into water, to destroy him. But if you can do anything, have compassion on us and help us. And Jesus said to him, “If you can! All things are possible for one who believes.” Immediately the father of the child cried out and said, “I believe; help my unbelief!”
translation.original_text: και πολλακις και εις πυρ αυτον εβαλεν και εις υδατα

translation.text: And when he had entered the house, his disciples asked him privately, “Why could we not cast it out?” And he said to them, “This kind cannot be driven out by anything but prayer.”
translation.original_text: και εισελθοντος αυτου εις οικον οι μαθηται αυτου επηρωτων αυτον

translator_notes.note: The father’s paradoxical confession—“I believe; help my unbelief”—acknowledges that faith and doubt coexist. Later manuscripts add “and fasting” to verse 29; Sinaiticus has only “prayer,” the more austere and likely original reading.
